data.title: Ross Stores: Solid Q3 Justifies Premium Valuation
data.description:
Shares of Ross Stores, Inc. (ROST) have been a solid performer over the past year, gaining 15%. During a difficult environment for many consumer-facing companies, Ross has benefited from its value-orientation as consumers (particularly at lower- and middle-income levels) carefully watch discretionary spending. This momentum continued in the third quarter, sending shares up 3% in late trading on Thursday. With updated financials and shares sitting near a 52-week high, now is a good time to see if Ross has further upside or if the good news is in the price.

In the company’s third quarter, Ross Stores earned $1.58, which beat expectations by $0.16 as revenue jumped 10% to $5.6 billion. Same-store sales were up an excellent 7%, aided by a strong back-to-school shopping season. The company continues to do an excellent job of appealing to shoppers even in a difficult retail environment, thanks to its value-orientation. Additionally, it has managed through tariff costs fairly well, despite the fact that apparel faces among the most tariff pressure of any category given how much is imported from Asia.

The company operates over 2,200 stores across 44 states with California, Texas, and Florida is primary exposures. Its flagship Ross Dress of Less brand is the nation’s largest off-price apparel retailer. As you can see below, its business mix is fairly diversified across men’s, women’s, children, and home goods. 70% of shoppers are women (both shopping for themselves and their family). It has a similar “treasure hunt” experience model, typically refreshing merchandise every month or so, leading to frequent visits by its core customer. Between the success at Ross and The TJX Companies (TJX), this has proven to be a durable and attractive retail model for both apparel and home goods.

Overall, there were about $0.05 of tariff costs in the quarter, as Ross was able to pass on much, but not all of its tariff costs. Gross margins were 28% in the quarter, down about 30bps from last year. Between concessions from supplies and price increases, Ross has been able to offset over 90% of tariff costs in my estimation. Barring a change in tariff policy, I expect tariff costs to peak in Q4/Q1. A potential Supreme Court ruling to overturn tariffs could alleviate this pressure, though the Trump Administration could seek to use other legal authority to impose other tariffs. For now, I assume existing tariffs remain in effect through the remainder of the Trump Administration.

SG&A was up 10.6% to $920 million. This was consistent with revenue growth and is due to its growing store count and ongoing wage and cost inflation. Overall, Ross generated an 11.6% operating margin, down 30bps from last year, given the lower gross margin. Still, the ability to retain margins despite cost pressures and a sluggish consumer environment has been very encouraging, speaking to ongoing market share gains.

Alongside results, the company raised its full year EPS guidance to $6.38-$6.46, which includes about $0.16 of net tariff costs. This guidance implies $1.77-$1.85 in Q4 EPS and same-store sales growth of 3-4% for the year. This outlook is consistent with the current $1.81 Q4 consensus and points to a fairly healthy holiday shopping season. To the extent consumers are pulling back, it is not apparent in Ross’s results.

As we think about the consumer spending outlook beyond just Q4, the “K-shaped” economic trend bears watching, where upper-income consumption outpaces spending by lower-income cohorts. According to Bank of America’s (BAC) analysis, we are seeing this play out. As you can see below, spending has diverged with higher-income spending accelerating over the past year while lower-income spending has decelerated. Spending across cohorts though has recovered over the past few months after the initial shock of “Liberation Day,” which caused extreme market volatility.

Given the fact lower income households face more pressure from inflation and have less benefit from strong asset prices, I do expect their spending to lag. That said, within constrained budgets, we may see spending shift to more off-price retailers, providing ample opportunity for ROST to gain wallet share. It is also interesting to note where this “K-shaped” divergence is largest. It is primarily in highly discretionary sectors, like travel and leisure. There is a gap in clothing and general merchandise, but the variance is more manageable.

Given its off-price focus, results thus far, and my view a recession is unlikely, I expect ROST to continue to generate sales growth in the current environment, primarily due market share gains. Additionally, I do expect lower and middle-income households to receive some benefit in Q1 from a larger tax refund season. Changes this year to tax on tips, overtime, charitable giving, and other measures do not impact this year’s withholding and instead will be felt via larger tax refunds. This should create a $15+ billion larger tax refund season. In essence, we have not felt the economic benefits of many of this year’s tax cuts, but that should help buoy spending in 2026. This is a key reason I do not see spending rolling over further.

As it continues to open new stores, Ross has been investing meaningfully in cap-ex, spending $619 million, up from $514 million last year. Even with this level of spending, the company has generated about $1.3 billion of free cash flow year to date, despite over $100 million of working capital pressures. Depending on Q4 working capital movements, Ross is well-positioned to generate over $1.7 billion of free cash flow this year. I expect cap-ex spending to remain elevated as management sees scope to grow its store count over time by about two-thirds from current levels. Over the past year, its store count is up 3.7% to 2,273. Barring a meaningful economic downturn, I expect mid-single-digit store count growth over the medium term.

Ross ended the quarter with $3.13 billion of inventory. This is up about 9.5% from last year, which is essentially in-line with its sales growth. This is a healthy level of inventory and does not leave the company exposed to having excess product, which can force a retailer to increase promotional activity, sacrificing margins.

Capital returns have been a critical part of the Ross Stores investment thesis. Importantly, it has a strong balance sheet, enabling ongoing returns of its free cash flow to shareholders. Ross ended the quarter with $4.1 billion of cash. Against this, it has $1.5 billion of debt and $3.7 billion of operating leases. With just ~$1 billion of net debt and debt-like instruments, the balance sheet is pristine and provides ROST with significant operating and financial flexibility.

Thanks to this, in the third quarter, Ross repurchased $262 million in stock, leaving it on track to spend $1.05 billion on buybacks this year, as previously communicated. Thanks to buybacks, its share count is down about 2% from last year. Aside from its buyback, it pays a 1% dividend yield. Given the health of its balance sheet, I see scope for higher buybacks next year after it completes its 2-year $2.1 billion buyback program.

Overall, Ross Stores continues to perform very well, and its off-price/value focus leaves it well positioned in this muted consumer spending environment. That said with shares at a 52-week high, much is in the price. Given modest sales growth and continued store openings, I am looking for the company to earn about $7 next year. That leaves shares with a ~23.5x multiple and a ~3.5% free cash flow yield. This is by no means a cheap value. However with 3-4% store growth and 2-3% same-store sales growth, we should see ~5-6% long-term growth.

This essentially paves the way for long-term returns in the 8-10% area (free cash flow yield plus growth). In other words, ROST is deserving of this premium multiple and should be able to deliver at least market-like (~8%) returns over the medium term. Plus with its pristine balance sheet and defensive macro positioning, its business model is durable, which should enable growing capital returns. As such, I view shares as a suitable Hold with Q3 results justifying the rally. I do not see enough upside to merit a buy rating (where I target a 10+% return), but on a pullback below $155, I would add Ross Stores.

data.title: Abrdn Life Sciences Investors Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund performance

The equity portion of the Fund rose (gross of fees) and outperformed its benchmark, the NASDAQ Biotechnology Index.1

The Fund outperformed its benchmark in the third quarter, driven by strong stock selection in biotechnology and therapeutics. Despite healthcare trailing broader markets due to rotation into cyclicals and AI-exposed technology, the Fund's focus on late-stage clinical catalysts and well- capitalised firms drove the outperformance. In terms of individual stocks, uniQure (QURE) advanced on renewed gene-therapy optimism, with progress in its CSL Behring partnership and programmes for Huntington's disease and epilepsy. Alnylam Pharmaceuticals (ALNY) rose on strong Amvuttra uptake and cardiovascular ribonucleic acid (RNA) platform updates, with reaffirmed growth targets. Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals (ARWR) benefited from positive data readouts and speculative mergers and acquisitions interest in the RNA sector.

Detractors included ARS Pharmaceuticals (ASRX), which lagged due to slower product launch metrics, and Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX), which saw profit-taking and competitive concerns in cystic fibrosis. Ultragenyx Pharmaceutical (RARE) weakened slightly due to longer regulatory timelines in rare diseases.

The latest available performance figures have been calculated net-of-fees in U.S. dollars for the period:

Cumulative and annualized total return as of September 30, 2025 (%)

Performance

NAV Market price
Quarter to date 27.50 24.72
Year to date 27.68 30.49
1 year 21.06 17.62
3 years (p.a.) 16.44 17.02
5 years (p.a.) 7.53 8.17
10 years (p.a.) 7.22 6.49
Past Performance is no guarantee of future results. Investment returns and principal value will fluctuate and shares, when sold, may be worth more or less than original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance quoted. NAV return data includes investment management fees, custodial charges and administrative fees (such as Director and legal fees) and assumes the reinvestment of all distributions. abrdn Inc. (the "Adviser") became the Fund's adviser on October 27, 2023. For periods prior to that date, the returns reflect performance information from a prior, unaffiliated adviser. The Fund is subject to investment risk, including the possible loss of principal. Returns for periods less than one year are not annualized.

Expense ratios

Fiscal year ended September 30 (%)

Annual Expenses Percent of Net Assets
Management Fees 1.04
Other Expenses 0.32
Operating Expenses 1.36
Effective upon the close of business on October 27, 2023, the Adviser entered into a written contract with the Fund to limit the total ordinary operating expenses of the Fund (excluding leverage costs, interest, taxes, brokerage commissions, acquired fund fees and expenses and any non-routine expenses) from exceeding 1.36% of the average daily net assets of the Fund on an annualized basis for twelve months (the "Expense Limitation Agreement"). The Expense Limitation Agreement may not be terminated before October 27, 2025, without the approval of the Fund's trustees who are not "interested persons" of the Fund (as defined in the 1940 Act).

Market review

US equities performed well over the quarter. The mid- August extension of the US-China tariff truce helped support sentiment, while new agreements with the European Union and Japan reduced tariffs on most of their products to 15%. However, optimism was tempered by President Trump's announcement of 100% tariffs on branded and patented pharmaceutical imports and higher duties on goods from Canada, India and Brazil, alongside expanded steel and aluminium tariffs. Monetary policy also shaped investor sentiment. The US Federal Reserve (Fed) lowered the federal funds rate by 25 basis points to 4.00%-4.25%, describing it as a "risk management" cut. Chair Jerome Powell reaffirmed the Fed's independence and commitment to returning inflation to target while noting signs of a softer labour market. The quarter ended with a partial US government shutdown after Congress failed to agree a funding deal, weighing on short-term growth and delaying key data releases.

The performance of the US healthcare sector notably lagged that of the broader US equity market over the quarter. Cyclical sectors such as communication services, information technology and consumer discretionary outperformed as investors rotated towards growth areas given softening economic data and slowing inflation, which fuelled expectations of interest-rate cuts. Materials underperformed the broader market despite improved US-China trade sentiment after the extension of a 90-day tariff freeze, as weakness in domestic industrial demand offset any benefit, although higher gold prices reflected ongoing economic and geopolitical uncertainty. In contrast, defensive sectors including consumer staples, utilities and real estate underperformed as risk appetite increased. Financials and industrials likewise underperformed, held back by softer economic data and waning business confidence. Energy broadly matched the performance of US equities, with oil prices ending flat as signs of softening demand, reflected in rising crude inventories, were offset by geopolitical tensions that raised supply concerns.

Activity

The Fund added positions in Jazz Pharmaceuticals (JAZZ), Arcus Biosciences (RCUS), Aquestive Therapeutics (AQST), Equillium (EQ), Shattuck Labs (STTK) and Apogee Therapeutics (APGE), targeting firms with strong balance sheets and near-term catalysts. Trims in Alnylam Pharmaceuticals and Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals locked in gains. Smaller biotechnology positions with matured theses or liquidity concerns were sold, increasing exposure to late- stage pipelines and diversifying the therapeutic mix.

Outlook & strategy

Uncertainty continues to cloud the macroeconomic, geopolitical and market outlook. A combination of shifting fiscal and monetary policies along with structural changes in global trade dynamics has created an increasingly volatile market environment. Much of the focus remains on the Trump administration's tariff strategy and its potential implications for economic growth and inflation. However, the broader macroeconomic backdrop is further complicated by labour-market data revisions, proposed tax cuts, widening budget deficits, evolving interest-rate policy, healthcare reform and a range of other policy initiatives that may have both intended and unintended consequences. Given the rapidly evolving landscape, market participants are struggling to build meaningful conviction around the trajectory of both domestic and international economies.

In general, the near- and long-term outlooks for healthcare companies remain favourable. Long-term demographic trends of an aging population should continue to support the growing demand for new healthcare products and therapies. Due to the healthcare sector's generally defensive characteristics, its relative underperformance in the short term should position it favourably, especially if the US and global economies slow down.

Footnotes

1 The NASDAQ Biotechnology Index is a modified capitalization-weighted index that includes securities of NASDAQ-listed companies classified according to the Industry Classification Benchmark as biotechnology or pharmaceuticals. For current holdings information, please visit abrdn Life Sciences Investors - Portfolio Holdings Aberdeen Group | Helping our customers achieve their financial goals

Important Information

in the healthcare industries, pharmaceuticals sector, biotechnology industry, managed care sector, life science and tool industry, healthcare technology sector, healthcare services sector, healthcare supplies sector, healthcare facilities sector, healthcare equipment sector, healthcare distributors sector, healthcare REIT, interest rate, credit/default, non-investment grade securities, key personnel, discount to NAV, anti-takeover provisions, related party transactions, non-diversification, government intervention, market disruption, geopolitical, and potential conflicts of interest. Effective immediately after the market close on 10/27/2023, abrdn Inc. became the Fund's investment manager. Please see the Fund's most recent annual report for more information on risks applicable to the Fund.

The Fund's investment return and principal value will fluctuate so that an investor's shares may be worth more or less than the original cost. Shares of closed-end funds may trade above (a premium) or below (a discount) the net asset value (NAV) of the Fund. The net asset value (NAV) is the value of an entity's assets less the value of its liabilities. The market price is the current price at which an asset can be bought or sold. There is no assurance that the Fund will achieve its investment objective. Past performance does not guarantee future results.
