data.title: HEICO Posts Big Margin Gains, But High Valuation Keeps Me At Hold
data.description:
HEICO Corporation (HEI, HEI.A) is a leading supplier in the aerospace and defense industry mostly known from its value creation through a combination of organic growth and successful integration of acquisitions. Despite strong growth prospects supported by industry tailwinds, I marked the stock a Hold in August. That has been shown to be the right call with a 0.4% increase in the stock price and a 3% increase in the share price. In this report, I discuss the company’s Q3 2025 earnings and update my price target.

HEICO Posts Remarkable Earnings

HEICO’s Q3 earnings were strong with 16% sales growth to $1.15 billion. This included 13% organic sales growth in the Flight Support Group and 7% organic growth in the Electric Technologies Group. It shows that the company’s sales and earnings growth is not solely driven by acquisitions, but the underlying business is growing as well. Operating income increased 22% to $265 million and EBITDA grew 21% to $316 million. Operating margins improved from 21.8% to 23.1% while EBITDA margins expanded from 26.4% to 27.6% despite acquisition related amortization.

In the Flight Support Group sales grew 18% to $802.7 million with operating income growing 29% to $198.3 million as margins expanded from 22.5% to 24.7%. The company saw strong performance in the parts business, but repair & overhaul sales also showed strong growth and specialty products grew low double digits. The company continues to enjoy strong aftermarket demand and demand for missile systems drove specialty product sales while commercial OEM sales are recovering slightly.

In the Electronics Technologies Group sales grew 10% to $355.9 million and operating income grew 6.9% to $81 million. The ETG business saw margins decline from 23.5% to 22.8%. The ETG segment margins are more susceptible to product mix and that is what showed in the third quarter.

What Are The Risks And Opportunities For HEICO?

HEICO is benefiting from strong demand for services and parts in the commercial airplane industry while we also see growth in defense driven by specialty products for missile systems and defense electronics. We note that the performance in FSG was exceptionally strong with EBITA margins of 27.3%, which does not provide an elevated base for modelling margins. The FSG business did benefit from an extremely positive mix during the quarter.

We remain mindful about that as well as the fact that growth is supported through a combination of aftermarket sales and OEM sales. Delays in production ramp up for commercial airplane programs do affect OEM sales but could positively impact aftermarket sales as airlines need to keep airplanes in service longer. That also means that as Boeing and Airbus bring production rates back up, we should be mindful of any margin pressure that may exist in the case that growth for aftermarket sales which are highly profitable slows and OEM sales which are lower margin pick up.

HEICO is known for growing businesses through acquisitions. Acquisitions always carry a degree of risk due to integration efforts required to lift margins. However, HEICO does have a solid track record on M&A, so I view that more as an opportunity rather than a risk. The company recently announced the acquisition of Axillion Fuel Containment which is the leading designer for fuel-cells for U.S. defense equipment. The company’s platforms include F-16, F-15, F/A-18, CH-47 Chinook, KC 135, B-52, AH-64 Apache, V-22 Osprey and the Bradley Fighting Vehicle. So, this a very defense focused acquisition for HEICO.

HEICO Stock Remains Expensive

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

HEICO has an attractive business. However, we also see that the company is trading at levels above the company’s median EV/EBITDA of around 28x and in turn that median value is above the peer group. For HEICO it does make sense to have a somewhat elevated EV/EBITDA as the company is rather heavy on acquiring company’s there by raising debt and mature the margins. However, currently we see no upside in the near future even with estimates having improved substantially. EBITDA estimates improved by 2.1% this year, 3.1% next year and 2.1% in 2027. That leads to a combined lift to expected EBITDA of 2.4% and the CAGR improving from 12.5% to 13.3%.

On free cash flow, expectations have increased by 7.9% for this year driven by strong results and lower capital expenditures. For the years after, the increases have been 4.8% and 4.3% for a total 5.5% increase. CAGR improved from 17.1% to 18.2%. Despite all of that, there is no substantial upside since the stock was already valued quite high and that means I am reiterating my Hold rating.

The key metrics show that the business is expected to perform very well with expanding margins, growing free cash flow and capital expenditures being less than 10% of operating cash flow. That leads to a substantial increase in the company’s cash position with little to no net debt on the books by 2027. This does create an opportunity for HEICO to acquire more companies without having a substantial increase in leverage compared to prior years which is definitely a positive. Absent of M&A opportunities, the company could return more value to shareholders. Where HEICO is lacking because it prioritizes M&A is shareholder returns and that also makes for a less attractive investing opportunity even when we keep in mind inorganic growth opportunities.

I believe that the company is executing well and likely will focus on M&A in the defense industry and that could potentially be a growth driver. However, at current prices everything we can see in terms of growth towards 2027 has already been priced in.

Conclusion: HEICO Has A Good Business, Valuation Not Attractive To Buy

I believe that HEICO is running the business well. The company is seeing good organic growth as well as inorganic growth with rising demand in the commercial airplane and defense industries. I do expect the company to further bolster its defense business going forward. However, despite all these positives we note that from valuation perspective there is no strong investment case and that is also partially driven by the fact that the company does not offer a compelling dividend. As a result, I believe HEICO is more of a long-term growth compounder rather than a strong investment opportunity at present.

data.title: Celanese: Getting Worse, Before Getting Better
data.description:
Early in the summer, I concluded that uncertainty prevailed in the case of Celanese Corporation (CE). That conclusion still seems valid today, with shares down another third to current lows around $36 per share.

The leveraged acquisition of DuPont's (DD) Materials & Mobility business left the business overleveraged at a good point in the cycle. With earnings coming down, the company resorted to a 95% dividend cut, idled production and cost saving measures, now complemented with divestment as well, to avoid a debt trap.

Some Perspective

Celanese used to be a pretty stable global chemical and special material business, this being a >$8 billion company in 2021. Two-thirds of this business was comprised out of acetyl chain operations, complemented by engineered materials, and some tiny other activities.

The business was very profitable, with EBITDA reported at $2.8 billion, with earnings reported around $17 per share. Trading around the $150 mark, shares traded at just 9 times earnings, but coming out of the pandemic, margins were reported at non-sustainable levels, with operating margins reported in the mid-twenties, far outpacing averages in the years before in the mid-teens.

Just at this point in time, a $21 billion valued business made a huge move, as Celanese paid $11 billion to acquire the mobility & materials business from DuPont, adding $3.8 billion in sales and $900 million in EBITDA, with these activities valued at premium multiples. Driven by a promise of synergies of half a billion, earnings were seen moving up to $20 per share, badly needed as leverage would jump to 4 times, all at a good point in the cycle.

Crashing Down

Aided by the acquisition, 2023 sales rose by 13% to nearly $11 billion, offsetting a huge decline in the organic performance of the business, with EBITDA reported down to $2.4 billion. This made that earnings power only came in at $9 per share, while net debt stood at nearly $12 billion. Somewhat surprising is that shares still traded around the $170 mark at the time.

The company triggered alarm bells in the third quarter of 2024, including a 95% cut in the dividend, idling of production, and implementation of costs saving measures, as shares fell a third overnight to $80 per share. This came after a big profit warning made that 2024 sales would fall another 6% to $10.3 billion, with earnings down to $8 and change, with EBITDA reported at just $2.4 billion. This was a modest number in relation to still a huge net debt load of $11.6 billion.

2025 - No Avail

After a soft first quarter, with revenues down to just $2.4 billion, and EBITDA reported at just $414 million, the company announced its intention to divest Micromax, its electronic ink and paste supplier. That was badly needed with net debt reported at $11.8 billion, all while tariffs made the world an even more uncertain pace, making it hard to be upbeat at $54 early in the summer.

Second quarter EBITDA was reported at $532 million on a $2.5 billion revenue base, yet continued weakness was seen. In fact, third quarter adjusted earnings were only seen between $1.10 and $1.40 per share, this comparing to a $1.44 per share number in the second quarter.

In October, the business announced its intention to close its acetate tow facility in Belgium, with 160 jobs being lost in the closing of this facility in 2026. Later that month, the company announced the divestment of Micromax to Element Solutions (ESI) in a $500 million cash deal, with the transaction involving some $40 million in EBITDA leaving the door, with the transaction seen closing in the first quarter of 2026.

In November, third quarter sales were reported at $2.4 billion, with adjusted earnings per share reported at $1.34 per share, that achieved on a $517 million EBITDA number. Amidst some cash flow generation, net debt fell to $11.4 billion, set to fall to $11 billion if we factor in the proceeds from Micromax. No avail is seen however, with fourth quarter earnings seen around $0.85-$1.00 per share, driven by continued weakness, in spite of all the cost saving measures.

And Now?

The big elephant in the room remains an $11.0 billion pro forma net debt level upon the Micromax sale, as the 110 million shares of the business grant equity a $4.0 billion equity valuation, and thus $15 billion enterprise valuation at face value of its debt.

This comes as the business is hurt by Chinese competition and exposure to automotive, with the company focusing heavily on internal manners to boost margins. Cost-saving measures and debt reduction initiatives are seen boosting 2026 earnings between a dollar or two next year, up from a current run rate close to $4-$5 per share.

Right now operating margins have fallen below 10%, as these are generally still good activities, but simply are overleveraged. Assuming a return to historical 15% margins on a subdued $10 billion revenue base, a $1.5 billion EBIT number in theory could yield about $800 million in pre-tax profits, or around $600 million, or $6 per share, after statutory taxes. If that happens, shares look very cheap from an earnings perspective, at just 6 times earnings.

The issue is that EBITDA trends at just $2 billion at best, as the business is very overleveraged from that point of view, for a leverage ratio around 5.5 times. In between all this, I believe that right now equity is a play ball of the change in debt, with debt being the dominant and emerging factor, and equity being the residual outcome.

While Celanese is fighting hard to retain competitiveness, the outlook for common equity holders remains uncertain and while near term liquidity seems solved, the company continues to walk a fine line in between depressed end markets.

From a countercyclical element, shares look reasonable and if things turn around, prospects for the shares look good, as shares could easily hit levels around the $100 mark, or more in a few year's time, but the potential outcome does not rule out very adverse scenarios as well.

Right now, the situation looks a lot more balanced, but real green shoots are still hard to be found here.

data.title: SP500: Good News For Nvidia Is Bad News For Everybody Else - Stay Short
data.description:
Key point:

The S&P500 (SP500) is trading a bubble valuation due to the valuations of mega-cap tech stocks under the AI theme. The AI bubble is in process of bursting, and the S&P500 is facing a key technical support, which once is broken is likely to trigger a much deeper selloff.

On 5Y chart S&P500 is right at the 20wma support. In 2022 and earlier in 2025 the 20wma breakdown led to 20%+ drawdown. This support is likely to be broken, despite Nvidia's (NVDA) blowout earnings report.

Nvidia's report is bad news

Nvidia reported earnings and beat on earnings and revenues, and boosted the revenue guidance - that's a triple beat.

We know that hyperscalers have been increasing AI capex, and thus, purchasing more Nvidia chips - that's what Nvidia's earnings reflect. But as I will explain this is not a goods news - it's actually is bad news for the market.

First, as hyperscalers are buying news chips, the older chips must face accelerated depreciation, while hyperscale's have been extending the useful life depreciation. Eventually, hyperscalers will have to accelerate depreciation and this will hit the earnings hard.

For Nvidia, it's good to sell more advanced chips on a 12–18-month schedule, but this is bad for everybody using these chips because of the depreciation issue.

Second, the ROR on Capex investment is likely negative. Thus, as hyperscalers increase Capex and buy more Nvidia chips, the higher the bad investment write-off will be. Further, given that more debt is used to finance AI capex, there is higher probability of defaults, for firms like Oracle (ORCL).

Thus, the fact that Nvidia continues to triple-beat points to a continuous irrational hype in the AI capex, which is likely to end badly in a major bubble bust.

Jensen Huang said this:

Blackwell sales are off the charts, and cloud GPUs are sold out. Compute demand keeps accelerating and compounding across training and inference — each growing exponentially. We’ve entered the virtuous cycle of AI. The AI ecosystem is scaling fast — with more new foundation model makers, more AI startups, across more industries, and in more countries. AI is going everywhere, doing everything, all at once.

Revenue shortfall and slow adoption

Here are the supporting facts for my thesis.

The only way that the depreciation issue could go away is if the earnings for hyperscalers (Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Meta (META), and foundational model developer OpenAI) significantly increase and ROR turns highly positive. For this to happen, the AI adoption must accelerate with almost all businesses fully scaling AI technology. That's what Huang is saying in the quote above: "AI is going everywhere, doing everything, all at once".

However, that's not really what's happening.

In September, Bain & Company’s 6th annual Global Technology Report said that "Even with AI-related savings, investors are still $800 billion short in annual revenue required to profitably fund the data centers of 2030".

This means that there is a significant revenue shortfall, and thus, the AI capex is unlikely to become profitable.

More importantly, McKinsey & Co state in The state of AI in 2025: Agents, innovation, and transformation that the AI adoption is still mostly in the pilot/experimental state - and that's three years after the ChatGPT revelation.

This clearly counters what Huang says. Yes, almost all companies are trying to implement AI technology, but the AI is not really adding much value.

Here are the details:

  • 88% of companies are using AI, but only 7% have it fully scaled, while 62% are stuck in experimental/pilot stage.

  • Even the agentic AI is not being scaled, the IT sector leads with 2% fully scaled, 8% scaling. That's not good.

  • Only 38% and 36% of companies state that AI improved costs, and profits respectively. This is also very low.

  • Further, the cost decrease is generally very modest at less than 10%.

  • In addition, the revenue increase is generally also very modest at less than 5%.

  • Other than a very small sample of AI high performers (7% of the companies), only 14% of companies see AI as a "transformative change" over next three years.

  • Inaccuracy continues to be a major concern, that's hallucinations.

McKinsey adds that: "The online survey was in the field from June 25 to July 29, 2025, and garnered responses from 1,993 participants in 105 nations representing the full range of regions, industries, company sizes, functional specialties, and tenures. Thirty-eight percent of respondents say they work for organizations with more than $1 billion in annual revenues. To adjust for differences in response rates, the data are weighted by the contribution of each respondent’s nation to global GDP."

Implications

So, these are the facts:

  • The AI capex is projected to keep increasing, possibly to trillions.
  • However, there is a significant revenue shortfall from this capex, suggesting that the ROR on AI capex is negative.
  • Nvidia's is selling the GPUs, that reflects the AI capex, so no surprise here.
  • However, the GPU buyers are facing accelerating depreciation schedule on these GPUs, which is likely to result in a major hit to earnings, and there are issues with debt financing.
  • The only way out of this situation is if the AI adoption sharply accelerates and makes the AI capex profitable.
  • However, the AI adoption seems to be stuck in the experimental/pilot stage, and it's not really effective in lowering costs or increasing revenues.
  • Put it all together, the AI bubble is likely to continue to deflate.
  • I didn't even discuss the circular, vendor financing issues.

Bottom line: The fact that Nvidia continues to sell GPUs to satisfy the AI capex demand is a bad sign for the market. Why? The AI capex is unprofitable.

Thus, the S&P500 could stage a technical short-covering rally from the key support level in response to Nvidia's earnings, but the AI bubble burst is likely to unfold.

  • Risk to the bearish thesis: Over the near term, the market will be influenced by sentiment, and the sentiment is still negative. However, we will not get more info about the financial results from the Mag 7 companies until the next earnings season, in March. Thus, the depreciation expense concerns could ease, and overall, the AI bubble concerns could ease. At this point, the market is not trading the recession theme, and broadly the credit risk is still very low. Thus, the sentiment could improve into the year-end, and the year-end rally is possible. The major risk to the bearish thesis is if the VIX drops below 20, and the 100dma support holds. At that point, the short position should be closed.

data.title: E3 Lithium: Massive Resource And Government Support, But There Are Concerns
data.description:
Over the years many people have asked me to write on E3 Lithium (OTCQX:EEMMF) and frankly I have been too busy. However, a friend asked me to kick the tires a bit. In this article we shall explore my initial thoughts on this lithium company.

A Rising Tide Lifts All Ships

When I look at E3 I see a very large project with very low lithium values. Large in the sense that the project is utterly massive in scope and it appears E3 is taking the Walmart (WMT) approach of scale, that is run many wells to capture low lithium values and then concentrate it. This can in fact work but let's look at that future demand before we get further into the project.

AI Data Center Mania And Lithium

The scope and scale of data center growth world-wide is large but still potentially in its infancy. If we look at various data centers we can see utterly massive projects. To put this into context a normal data center can range from power use of 5 mega watts to on the larger side 100 mega watts. Some pending and proposed data centers are going massive in scale at 1,000 MW and one proposed data center in Roswell, NM aims to ramp up to over 4,000 megawatts.

The amount of power required is utterly massive. Where is all this demand coming from? The big boys like Oracle (ORCL), Meta (META), CoreWeave (CRWV) along with AMD (AMD) and Nvidia (NVDA) are all pushing various aspects of datacenters.

Why It Matters

In order to power data centers you need steady reliable power but you also need energy storage in case Mr. Murphy strikes and the power is cut off. This power storage can come in many flavors such as diesel back up generators, underground natural gas caverns, or even lithium power banks and this brings us back to E3. E3 could meet some of this future lithium power storage demand if all goes well.

Drones, Data Centers, And EV

When you ponder the world of the future, what might it look like? Obviously the military is going to continue to produce and gravitate to more drones now that the masses have watched the Ukraine-Russia war. Many of these drones will require lithium. Land warfare might change slightly as well. China is exploring diesel-electric hybrid options, and the US might be introducing a modified Abrams battle tank (Called the AbramsX) with a small lithium battery in it. Why introduce a lithium battery? Easy, you can operate cold and silent, but I digress. Getting back on point, if the future is more drones, more data centers and more EV's we might assume all of this is going to require additional lithium. This puts us back to E3 Lithium. Out of a multitude of lithium stocks is this the chosen one?

Concerns About E3 Lithium And Risk

Frankly, I do not like the low lithium values of 74.5 mg/l that E3 sports. To put things into context Albemarle (ALB) has values of around 120 mg/l for Clayton Valley. Meanwhile, Arkansas values range from 200 mg/l to mid 600 mg/l as you enter into Standard Lithium's (SLI) Franklin Texas project. Something can be said about high grades but on the flip side something can be said about taking the Walmart approach by running many wells that extract lithium to be concentrated. It can work, however it most likely will be costly. The capital costs of the project are on the very high side with CAPEX at $2.465 billion. OPEX is on the high side as well at $7,250 a tonne of lithium (and with inflation could run higher).

The project economics are based on lithium hydroxide establishing a long-term price of $31,344 a tonne. Yes, this is a possible long-term price, but I would have liked to see this number more in line with the mid $20k range which the industry seems to use for lithium carbonate. For example Lithium Americas (LAC) used $24,000 a tonne. Century Lithium (OTCQX:CYDVF) also uses $24,000 a tonne. Meanwhile Standard Lithium (SLI) pegged long-term lithium prices for its study at $22,400.

E3 Lithium has its long-term price estimate that is 30.6% higher than comparable averages which might make some of the project numbers seem better than could be expected.

Note: Per my search Lithium hydroxide is currently quoted at $10,959 USD a tonne in China and lithium carbonate goes for slightly higher at $12,348 as of November 17, 2025. E3's study is based on lithium hydroxide, but they do have plans to shift to lithium carbonate but more on this shortly.

E3 Lithium Financials

Pulling up the Q2 financials, noted in CDN (thousands): $7,386 in cash and $2,061 in accounts receivable vs current liabilities of $1,398 and current lease obligations of $236 and additional lease obligations of $600. We can see that a burn rate is present but on a good note accounts payable did decrease.

We can calculate the burn rate here at about $2.2 CDN to $2.5 CDN million a quarter give or take.

We can back out $648k CDN of share based compensation for Q2 2025 or $1.5 CDN million for the six months ending as these are just paper losses. This gives us a better idea of the true burn rate which is about $1.2 CDN to $1.35 CDN million per quarter. Thus with the cash injection noted below they can continue for quite some time.

E3 Closes Financing

On a positive note the company announced that it closed its equity offering of $12.2 CDN. This restocks the company treasury.

E3 Sells Assets Saskatchewan Assets

On November 20, 2025, the company announced it has sold one of its non-core assets in Saskatchewan for $6 million CDN (or $4.296 USD). I think this makes some sense as really they have plenty of assets elsewhere. The market does not appear to be a fan of this event as the stock is down -3.93% on the US ticker. If we combine the totals we see almost $9.4 million CDN in cash followed by $12.2 million CDN via the equity offering and now an additional $6 million CDN. Grand total is $27.6 million CDN. Given the burn rate it appears they can continue for quite some time.

Conclusion

While I hold reservations about this project (and I do believe that better projects exist), I am unable to ignore that the Canadian government is supporting the project. It is very possible that the powers that be in Canada will accept some challenging project economics and very high OPEX in a bid to advance a very large project (with a very long life) to advance lithium mining independence.

E3 Lithium also seems to be exploring the idea of shifting from lithium hydroxide to the superior priced (and gaining market share) lithium carbonate. Additionally, my contacts in the industry give the management team an A rating. That is notable.

At a market cap of just $56 million USD, something could be said of buying a small very speculative position just to have some exposure. If additional government support or a lithium super cycle arrives the shares could rise in value to mimic prior cycles.

Yet, I do think this speculative project is going to have some challenges obtaining total funding or even partial funding (if they go forth with talk of adopting a phased approach).

Money could be made here but as my friend Peter The Great said "Easier money can be made elsewhere". If we hit a lithium super cycle a massive rising tide could lift all lithium ships including this one. I am going to rate this venture as "neutral and take a wait and see approach" to "Open a very small position and see how it progresses".

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Micron Technology: From Commodity To AI Powerhouse
data.description:
Thesis

I see a bullish outlook for Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) that is centered on its transformation from a commodity memory producer into a robust growth engine powered by accelerating AI-driven demand in memory and data center infrastructure. I expect this massive expansion of AI data centers to create an insatiable need for high-performance dynamic random access memory. Further, I believe this will drive higher, more sustainable pricing and ultimately deliver robust margins. Given its strong momentum and competitive product portfolio, I view Micron as one of the best ways to gain direct exposure to the current AI and data-center boom.

Company Overview

Micron Technology is the largest U.S.-based memory manufacturer that specializes in a comprehensive portfolio that includes dynamic random access memory, NAND flash memory, and high-bandwidth memory ("HBM"). This distinction as the only major American memory producer gives the company a unique geopolitical advantage, particularly with the large-scale domestic manufacturing investments supported by the CHIPS Act initiatives, which I view as a powerful, long-term structural tailwind.

Pivoting to high-value products is paying off for MU. Fiscal Q4 2025 results greatly exceeded my expectations with revenue hitting a record of $11.32 billion, and Non-GAAP EPS came in at $3.03, far surpassing the consensus estimate.

Strategic Direction and HBM Roadmap

Strategically, Micron is backing this growth with a significant DRAM-focused CapEx plan of approximately $18 billion through fiscal 2026.

In a move to optimize its portfolio, Micron has confirmed plans to exit the lower-margin managed NAND business. This allows for reallocation of resources toward high-value data center NAND SSDs and expanding segments, such as automotive storage solutions. Regarding the future of memory, the company is innovating rapidly in HBM4, planning to use an in-house advanced CMOS process for certain components while partnering with TSMC (TSM) to produce the base logic die for the HBM4E generation.

News And Catalyst for Micron Technology

Accelerating AI Demand and Market Shifts

Micron expects HBM demand to unlock multi-year growth potential and sees the High-Bandwidth Memory Total Addressable Market growing aggressively to exceed $100 billion by 2030. This makes the HBM market alone potentially larger than the entire DRAM industry in 2024. Furthermore, DDR5 pricing has surged, and DRAM prices are expected to rise between 15% to 20%.

Exceptional Stock Performance

Micron shares have seen a surge of 123.18% year-to-date, with 48.07% over the past month. This performance has far outpaced broader market indexes, along with most technology peers. Furthermore, Renaissance Technologies initiated a new position in Micron during the third quarter. I see this as a strong signal of institutional confidence in Micron Technology’s long-term trajectory. Furthermore, the company continues to return capital to shareholders, and it recently declared a quarterly dividend of $0.115 per share.

Micron Technology Financial Overview

A review of Micron Technology's recent financial performance reveals it is exhibiting strong top-line momentum and industry-leading margins. This has earned it an overall Profitability Grade of A+.

MU’s financial results show significant operational leverage and a powerful rebound in earnings, which gives me confidence in a bullish outlook. The Trailing Twelve Months ending in August 2025 saw the company report total revenues of $37.37 billion. Further, the latest reported Normalized EPS of $3.03 beat the consensus estimate by $0.17.

Despite the TTM Gross Profit Margin of 39.79% sitting below the Information Technology sector median of 49.79%, I see the company excelling in higher-level profitability metrics. Micron Technology reported a robust TTM EBITDA of $18 billion and an EBITDA Margin of 48.40%, which is much higher than the sector median of 10.68%. The Net Income Margin of 22.84% is far higher than the sector median of 4.67%. These factors help to validate a bullish view of the company’s efficiency in controlling costs.

Growth Profile

Micron's recent growth figures paint a clear picture of a company in a vigorous expansion phase. This is reflected in its overall A+ Growth Grade. The year-over-year metrics are exceptionally strong as well. The company has a year-over-year Revenue Growth of 48.85%. Furthermore, this growth is accelerating profit generation, shown by the EBITDA Growth of 100.93%.

Additionally, I notice the company backing this financial growth with aggressive capital investment. Micron Technology has shown CapEx growth of 89.09%. The growth in investments is likely focused on high-demand technology, such as High-Bandwidth Memory. This is essential for the AI sector. Additionally, the company is seeing a demand increase.

Balance Sheet and Cash Flow Analysis

The company reported strong liquidity with Total Cash & Short Term Investments amounting to $10.3 billion. The company does carry a Total Debt of $15.3 billion, but its leverage appears manageable with an Equity ratio of 28.34%. This allows it to have flexibility to fund operations along with future investments.

The cash flow statement, however, presents a nuanced view that I have to address. The Cash from Operations remains strong with an inflow of $17.5 billion for the trailing twelve months. Yet, the investment into production capacity shows in the Net Capital Expenditure of $15.8 billion. This heavy spending results in a TTM Levered Free Cash Flow Margin of -2.38%.

While a negative FCF is typically viewed as a drawback, in Micron's context, I believe it reflects an intentional, heavy front-loading of capital for expansion. This is necessary to capitalize on the next wave of demand, rather than an inability to generate cash from core business operations.

Calculating A Price Target

The overall Valuation grade for MU comes in at a moderate C+, which I feel helps to set the stage for the rest of the assessment. On a trailing twelve-month basis, the GAAP P/E ratio is at around 32.52x, which initially looks expensive compared to the sector median. However, when I look at the forward P/E Non-GAAP, it drops dramatically to $14.70. This earns it an attractive A- grade. This stark difference between the high trailing multiple and low forward multiple is the market hinting that it has high expectations for future earnings growth. As a result, investors are willing to pay a premium for the future earnings growth.

Since Micron’s story is focused on future performance, it is important to focus on the relevant consensus earnings for the future year. In particular, the FY 2027 EPS estimate of $18.83. I will then apply two different P/E multiples to estimate a conservative and upside scenario.

To calculate the conservative price target, I assume the market maintains its current discounted valuation multiple relative to its growth profile. I use the current FY 2026 Forward P/E of 14.68. I then multiply this by the FY 2027 EPS of $18.83, which yields a conservative price of $276.45.

Second, I calculate the Upside Price Target. This assumes the market eventually re-rates Micron to match the broader Information Technology sector median multiple, which I feel is entirely justified by their superior growth and profitability grades. I use the Sector Median Forward P/E of 23.54. This results in multiplying $18.83 by 23.54, which results in an upside price of $443.20. Considering the price of the stock is currently at $241.95, it suggests that there is substantial upside.

Risks to Micron Technology

Competitive and Supply Pressures

I am concerned regarding competition in the HBM market with SK Hynix (OTCPK:HXSC.F) and Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) aggressively ramping up capacity. Samsung is rapidly seeking to close the gap and secure major next-generation HBM contracts. I see this as posing a major strategic threat to Micron's gains.

Management has highlighted ongoing supply constraints in DRAM and NAND, noting that meaningful incremental supply is difficult to bring on. While this is currently a positive for pricing power, if Micron cannot meet key customer demand for next-generation products due to these constraints, I recognize it risks losing long-term market share to better-supplied rivals.

Geopolitical and Trade Risks

China’s stricter rare earth export rules have the potential to cause major problems along the supply chain for semiconductors. This may potentially impact the cost and availability of the materials for advanced memory manufacturing.

Micron plans to stop supplying server chips to data centers in mainland China after failing to recover from a 2023 government ban on its products in critical Chinese infrastructure. Although the company is diversifying its customer base, mainland China generated $3.4 billion, or 12% of its total revenue, in the last fiscal year, and I consider this exit a permanent loss of access to a large, fast-growing domestic data center market.

Conclusion

Micron Technology stands at a profound inflection point where it is evolving from a structurally cyclical memory player into a growth powerhouse. It is playing a direct role in enabling the global AI economy. The cornerstone of my bullish thesis is the accelerating demand for high-performance memory, particularly High-Bandwidth Memory, which I believe promises more sustainable pricing and margins.

Micron’s strategic pivot toward high-value products is vividly reflected in its record earnings and A+ Growth Grade, confirming to me that they are positioned to capitalize on the AI and data-center boom. While I recognize that aggressive capital expenditure to secure HBM capacity results in near-term negative free cash flow, I view this as a necessary, strategic front-loading of investment to secure market leadership. Despite competition and geopolitical risks, I find the company’s future earnings potential compelling. The sharp reduction in the forward P/E and my calculated Upside Price Target of $443.20 solidifies my conviction that Micron is a Strong Buy for gaining direct, long-term exposure to this generational technology.

data.title: ETV: Discounted Call Writing CEF For Monthly Pay
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

Our last focus piece on the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Opportunities Fund (ETV) was in the market slump earlier in 2025. This was primarily a tech slump heading into the year, and then tariffs were announced, sending the entire market lower but seeing a quick recovery. Since then, we've seen strong performance from this call writing closed-end fund, but despite that, the fund has seen its discount widen a bit further since that time as well. I believe that continues to make it an attractive choice for a tech-leaning portfolio that pays monthly.

ETV Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: -2.53
  • Discount/Premium: -8.23%
  • Distribution Yield: 8.55%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.08%
  • Leverage: N/A
  • Managed Assets: $1.809 billion
  • Structure: Perpetual

ETV's investment objective is to "provide current income and gains, with a secondary objective of capital appreciation." To achieve this, the fund will invest "in a diversified portfolio of common stocks and writes call options on one or more U.S. indices on a substantial portion of the value of its common stock portfolio to seek to generate current earnings from the option premium."

Performance - Discount Remains Appealing

Since our last update early in the year, the fund's total returns have been fairly attractive. However, the fund has lagged the S&P 500 Index. For having even more tech-leaning exposure compared to the major market index, that can be disappointing.

One of the factors here is that the call writing strategy limits upside potential in a sharply rallying market. While we saw a significant slump shortly after the last piece was posted, there was a very sharp recovery that followed that. ETV is overwritten by nearly 100% and writes pretty close to the money options. In fact, the latest shows they are writing right at the money. They write options against the S&P 500 Index and the Nasdaq 100 Indexes rather than underlying portfolio positions.

That said, we've also seen the fund's discount slip wider once again. Going from closer to a 4% discount to pushing over 8%. That means the actual underlying portfolio performed a few percentage points better on an absolute basis than the actual share price. That's where I believe the fund still remains an opportunity. We also made a quick update on this fund more recently, in September—and the discount has widened a touch further since that time as well.

A wider discount for ETV has become relatively normal in the last several years. It primarily stemmed from the steep drop in 2023 when they adjusted their distribution lower. Unlike many ETFs, CEFs often pay a level distribution for lengthy periods of time. When they cut those distributions, even if more or less warranted, investors often flee. We then see the types of drops we had with ETV. Sometimes the discount recovers, and sometimes it doesn't, but I still believe either way that ETV remains an attractive fund to consider.

However, it is worth noting the number of call writing ETFs that have launched in the last several years. That's likely to have taken out some of the dollars that would go toward CEFs and get funneled into those new offerings. Where ETV can still have the edge is playing the discount/premium mechanic inherent in the CEF structure. ETFs don't have that because of the creation/redemption mechanism in the structure that makes the share price and NAV discount/premiums negligible.

While ETV is certainly attractive on a relative valuation basis to its historical level, it isn't all good news. Where I may be a bit more hesitant with adding too aggressively is that the overall market appears relatively stretched on a historical basis. The market is priced for perfection on the back of primarily the Magnificent 7 names, which could lead to greater potential volatility in the future if they start to slip.

We've already seen a big drop from Meta Platforms (META) in their latest earnings after increased capex turned investors off. META is a top 10 portfolio holding for ETV, so they would have participated in some of this downside. Fortunately, Amazon (AMZN), another top holding for ETV, was able to see a nice share price pop to help balance that. Still, the META performance highlights the risks to the downside, while AMZN highlights just how volatile things can get, even to the upside—though that has even come down quite sharply more recently from its latest pop.

Distribution - Monthly Pay

An appealing aspect of many CEFs (and ETFs) is the monthly distributions that they can spin off to investors. Some ETFs have now even begun doing weekly payouts—which can be appealing to investors and again siphon out some dollars toward those ETFs rather than investing in CEFs.

With that said, all these call writing funds will rely on capital gains to fund their payouts, whether it is from underlying portfolio appreciation or the option premiums generated from writing the calls. Over time, it can require adjustments in the case of ETV. We can see that over the years, net investment income for ETV is very limited, which is due to the lean toward a more tech-oriented portfolio that doesn't pay or pays rather limited yields.

For the ETFs, they typically pay varying rates with each payout, so one is never quite sure what they may be getting until it is announced. Some investors don't mind the varying payout, and I own a number of those ETFs these days myself. However, some investors still find comfort in knowing what the payout will be for a relatively longer period of time.

Further, this is where a CEF's discount can add another 'bonus' once again. This is because the fund's distribution rate on a share price basis currently comes out to 8.55%, while at the same time, the NAV distribution rate comes in at 7.85%. That means that the underlying portfolio has to only generate 7.85%, but investors purchasing shares today get a bit of a bonus due to the discount.

For tax purposes, we touched on that in our prior update. Here is the recap:

For tax purposes, ETV has historically classified larger portions of its distribution as return of capital. However, that can fluctuate from year to year depending on what the managers do in terms of realizing gains or not.

For 2024, we saw a fairly minimal amount designated as ROC. At the same time, the distribution was still quite tax-friendly as the remainder was primarily long-term capital gains and even a small portion of qualified dividends.

ETV's Portfolio

We already mentioned the option writing strategy and included the latest statistics on that—that is the more "exciting" part of the portfolio, as it is the active part of the fund's strategy. Even that isn't all that complicated, as the fund simply rolls the trades every two weeks, adjusting the strike price as necessary. So overall, the fund takes a rather mechanical approach that isn't overly complicated.

In looking at the portfolio, the fund invests in a blended S&P 500 and Nasdaq approach, which is why the fund lines up somewhere in the middle in terms of the tech exposure. The SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) is about ~36.5% tech, and the Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ) is around 55.5%.

This is, of course, driven by the mega-cap tech names that dominate these indexes—and in mirroring that, ETV as well. Though they do this with only 144 holdings compared to the ~500 in the S&P 500 and the ~100 in the Nasdaq 100.

Since they write options against indexes rather than underlying holdings, the fund being positioned as it is makes sense. When writing index options, one is essentially writing naked calls because an index can't be owned directly. With ETV investing in a mixture of holdings leaning toward the indexes that it's writing against, in an indirect way, it becomes "covered."

That creates a situation where it is highly unlikely that the fund can experience unlimited losses. Unlimited losses are possible because, in theory, an index can rise to infinity—but we know in reality that won't happen. What can happen is a sharp move higher, where losses can be substantial when they go to roll their call options. Given that the underlying portfolio reflects the underlying index (owning at least all the important names that are going to make the biggest moves), they can experience losses on their short calls, but that is offset by appreciation in the portfolio.

Conclusion

ETV continues to trade at an attractive discount. In fact, the discount has become even more appealing. While there are a number of competitors in the ETF space that are now available and doing a similar strategy of writing calls, the discount adds appeal that isn't available in the ETF structure.

By investing in ETV, one can own the same underlying portfolio but at a discounted price. This also means the distribution rate on a share price basis is higher relative to what the underlying portfolio has to produce in terms of returns.

On the CEF/ETF front. If Eaton Vance asked my opinion on what they could do with ETV, I'd suggest lowering the management fee (which they won't unless forced to because it is 'closed' capital), but it could make it a bit more competitive. Since that isn't likely, during market pullbacks, reduce the amount of the portfolio that is overwritten with short calls—to anticipate a recovery that has pretty consistently happened historically. By reducing the notional value that they are overwritten, it takes a more active approach similar to how NEOS applies its options writing strategy. Another, perhaps more extreme step, but in addition to the ones above, would be to let shareholders vote to convert the fund to an ETF. But I digress.

With ETV, there are some risks, as always. There is no guarantee the discount may narrow again, or the discount could go even wider. One catalyst for widening discounts could be a broader market sell-off. During times of volatility, we generally see discounts widen in the CEF space more broadly.

With a market looking stretched overall, a market correction wouldn't be surprising. They are a normal part of investing, but they can become more volatile the higher the valuation and expectations that investors have.

data.title: Archer-Daniels-Midland: Despite Another Guidance Cut In Q3, It Shows Resilience
data.description:
Intro

If an investor looks at Archer-Daniels-Midland Company (ADM), it doesn’t offer any standout, eye-catching positives that would make it an outright attractive investment—at least at first glance. At the time of writing this analysis, ADM trades at a P/E multiple of around 17 and offers a dividend yield of 3.5%. Solid, balanced figures that don’t indicate either overvaluation or undervaluation, in short, nothing particularly spectacular. However, in today’s turbulent markets, where some capital flows are rotating out of tech and into more defensive stocks, in my opinion, ADM deserves a closer look. As one of the largest players in its field globally and a very long-time member of the S&P 500, it stands out as a resilient option that could potentially add much-needed protection and diversification for investors' portfolios.

Having completed this analysis, I rate ADM as a Buy. While the valuation approach I used suggests ADM is fairly valued, I believe the current market rotation toward more defensive stocks could offer additional support for ADM’s share price. Notably, the third downward revision in outlook — and the stock’s resilient behavior following it — indicates that much of the downside risk may already be priced in. Furthermore, greater clarity on China trade relations and upcoming regulatory decisions regarding biofuels policy are likely to provide further support once resolved.

Archer-Daniels-Midlands In Brief

The history of ADM dates back to 1902, when George A. Archer and John W. Daniels founded a linseed crushing business. The company’s proud history, shaped by remarkable individuals and significant achievements, certainly deserves a more detailed overview — which can be explored here in greater depth.

The company currently presents itself as follows:

Headquartered in Chicago, Illinois, ADM has 44,000 colleagues who serve customers across six continents, generating revenue of $86 billion in 2024.

We're a global leader in human and animal nutrition and the world's premier agricultural origination and processing company. Our breadth, depth, insights, facilities and logistical expertise give us unparalleled capabilities to meet needs for food, beverages, health and wellness and more. From the seed of the idea to the outcome of the solution, we enrich the quality of life the world over.

ADM operates a vast global infrastructure, comprising around 270 production facilities and 420 crop sourcing locations across 60 countries. Its integrated logistics network spans barges, trucks, railcars, and deep-sea vessels — enabling efficient movement of goods across continents.

The company’s operations are structured into three reportable segments: Ag Services and Oilseeds, Carbohydrate Solutions, and Nutrition.

Put simply, my generalization is that ADM acts as an extension of farmers — purchasing their crops, providing know-how on what and how to grow, and supplying essential inputs. In addition to serving as a major crop trader, ADM transforms raw materials sourced from growers into hundreds of products tailored to global demand — ranging from starches for the packaging industry to environmentally friendly aviation fuel additives, among others.

Recent Developments

From early 2022 to 2024, the stock hovered between $70 and $100. Following the January 2024 selloff, caused by an accounting scandal, it has since traded within a $40–$65 range. The current price of $58 sits near the upper end of this recent trading corridor, despite a downward revision to the 2025 outlook just a few weeks ago.

Q3 Earnings

In Q3, ADM reported non-GAAP EPS of $0.92, beating estimates by $0.07. Revenue came in at $20.37 billion, up 2.2% year-over-year, but fell short of expectations by $400 million.

The company provided a summary of its quarterly performance as follows:

The primary source of revenue $15.6 billion— accounting for 77% of the total — came from the Ag Services & Oilseeds segment. Within that, Ag Services contributed $10.3 billion, representing approximately 50% of ADM’s overall revenues:

The operating profit breakdown prints a different story — while Ag Services & Oilseeds remains the most profitable segment, contributing 45% of the total $845 million (before corporate and one-time expenses) operating profit, the Carbohydrate Solutions segment, despite generating roughly six times less revenue, follows closely with 40%. The Nutrition segment accounts for the remaining 15%:

Outlook

The outlook was revised downward for the third time this year:

Management highlighted the key headwinds related to biofuels, which led to the downward revision of the outlook. Still, they provided a more optimistic view considering 2026:

Given the deferral in U.S. biofuel policy and other global movements, it is difficult to predict the timing of when we will see a structural increase in biofuel demand. As a result, we are lowering our expectations for full year 2025.

Overall, the recent progress with the trade deal with China, coupled with our expectation of gaining U.S. biofuel policy clarity within the next several weeks or months is an encouraging setup for next year. We expect 2026 will offer a more constructive environment for both the industry and the American farmer..

Additional Highlights from Earnings Call

The positives were pointed out by management:

Our Ag Services subsegment executed a robust export program during the quarter, supported by strong corn and meal programs. We achieved the best total export volume for the month of September since 2016

..and the negatives:

Crushing: Operating profit declined 93% in 3Q25 vs 3Q24, primarily driven by lower margins resulting from deferred biofuel policy and global trade challenges. 3Q25 results also reflected a net favorable timing impact of $41 million and a net $23 million decrease in insurance proceeds relative to 3Q24

Starches and Sweeteners (S&S): Operating profit decreased 36% in 3Q25 vs 3Q24, as global volumes and margins were impacted primarily by lower demand. S&S margins also continued to be impacted by persisting higher corn costs in EMEA related to corn quality issues. 3Q25 includes a net decrease of $42 million of insurance proceeds compared to 3Q24.

Management also highlighted the widely discussed changes in consumer behavior:

This is a continuation of consumer buying trends we have been experiencing throughout 2025 with softness in demand in sweeteners and a reduction in starches demand primarily from less consumption of packaged goods and corrugated paper.

The CEO provided a great summary of the main drivers behind commodity prices that are relevant to ADM’s business:

I think that at this point in time, the 2 big events that will move commodity prices will be clarity on the China trade deal and regulatory clarity on the biofuels policy.

Longer-term Positives Highlighted:

we still feel very strong about 2026. All these things that are under -- in motion right now, whether it's the RVO finalization, the RVO is positive for domestic feedstocks and certainly, the trade deal potentially to have more sales to China is also positive.

In my view, it’s worth mentioning the solid cash flow generation and stability, which have remained nearly consistent with last year:

Thanks to strong cash flow, ADM has repaid debt, bringing its net debt/EBITDA multiple down to 1.8x — a level that comfortably supports both continued investment in business growth and dividends. Given the company’s solid financial position, it seems reasonable to expect a 25th consecutive dividend increase next year.

Valuation

Picking the right valuation method for ADM is quite a challenge, given the company’s highly volatile earnings driven by the cyclical nature of agricultural commodities. I tend to favor a price-to-free cash flow approach when the company has a low net debt level and a type of business that aligns well with such modeling. In my view, when profitability metrics are subject to above-average volatility — compounded by numerous one-off items and exceptional adjustments in addition to cyclicality— cash flow ultimately provides a clearer picture of underlying performance. In ADM’s case, a net debt/EBITDA multiple of 1.8 and while volatile still consistently positive annualized free cash flow in recent years, supporting this approach.

I took the last three trailing annualized, rounded free cash flow figures from the same periods ending in September, summed them, and divided by three to arrive at an average. This resulted in an estimated annual free cash flow of $2.9 billion. Applying a conservative P/FCF multiple of 10x implies a fair market capitalization of approximately $29 billion. Given that ADM’s current market cap as of November 20 stands at around $28 billion, I conclude that the stock is more or less fairly valued at the ~$58 level.

Risks

As mentioned in the introduction, the stock selloff in 2024 was triggered by an accounting scandal. While it's no longer the central talking point, there remains a possibility that further developments could surface, potentially affecting the company’s reputation and business operations. Additionally, as previously noted, another key risks include China trade relations and various regulatory matters, with biofuels policy currently standing out as the most impactful.

Takeaway

While Archer-Daniels-Midland Company is certainly not the most boring company for investors to own — in fact, its past adventures were compelling enough to inspire a movie — the business itself operates on a massive scale and, in my view, is well diversified across a range of agricultural commodities and the products made from them.

One might assume its operations are highly cyclical, and they are — but ADM’s strength lies in the breadth of its exposure. Thanks to its scale, the company experiences different cycles across various crops, creating a form of diversified cyclicality. In short, strong cycles often offset weaker ones, providing meaningful risk balancing across ADM’s business.

While there's no guarantee that ADM won't offer more material fit for movie scripts, the company's fundamentals are undeniably strong — and with high-level execution, patient investors could potentially be well rewarded here.

data.title: Utz Brands: A Regional Engine Quietly Scaling Into A National Snack Platform
data.description:
I see Utz Brands (UTZ) stock as cheap because the company continues to execute well across its core brands, gain share market by market, and expand margins through ongoing supply-chain improvements. The valuation also remains inexpensive compared with packaged-food peers, as the market continues to price Utz at a discount to its improving returns and earnings quality.

Business Quality: Regional Trust Converted into Scalable Infrastructure

Utz is one of the oldest operating brands in the salty snack category. For many years, its success factor had indeed been extremely localized: dense route delivery network, extensive relationships with major retailers, and high repeat business in the Mid-Atlantic region. UTZ is now seeking to leverage these strengths to create a new platform based on four scalable brands: UTZ, Zapp's, On the Border, and Boulder Canyon.

It’s not just about processing more volume for Utz. It’s about route density, operational efficiency, and executing at the store level, attributes that take years to build and are inherently difficult to replicate. Its distribution network, comprised of direct-store delivery and independents, also offers visibility into its stores, something not feasible for a warehouse-based model. It’s their inherent advantage to maintain their stores’ shelves in model condition while expanding geographically.

Rather, management’s shift in direction after their IPO has proved to be strategic in nature, rather than simply cosmetic. Rather than acquiring several small regional brands, Utz invested in helping those few to have nationwide appeal, while shedding those with lower ROI, optimizing their plant locations for improved efficiency, in preparation for their entrance into new markets outside their traditional home. Of course, such necessary changes took some significant time, during which their growth did slow somewhat.

Positioning For Uncertainty: Share Gains in A Fragmenting Category

The salty snack category has transitioned from being pandemic-demand driven to more normalized, with softer sales and more private-label share. In this environment, Utz has continued to grow, but it has also continued to increase share at retail, especially in markets with no previous footprint.

Among the most significant trends is Utz’s support for demand without disrupting its overall pricing structure. Rather than opt for regular pricing reductions, Utz employs strategic promotion mechanisms such as large value packs, which support statutory prices on-shelf while protecting overall market share. Since such promotion mechanisms are financially driven by productivity improvements rather than adjustments in overall pricing, these support consumer value for market share during times of inflation or competitiveness.

Its expansion in California demonstrates such discipline. Rather than opting for drastic promotion-entry strategies, Utz’s plan for expansion in new regions involves the acquisition of routes, and such is based on its success in other regions. Indeed, it is one of the largest markets for salty snack foods in America, simply contributing to its significance, given its size, no matter the level of market penetration, especially for companies with such growth prospects in market share on national markets.

Financials: What The Numbers Reveal

Utz begins 2025 with financial foundations appearing to be humble, but there is substantial, measurably improved underlying performance. Overall financial performance for 2024 indicated solid base business growth in branded snack products, coupled with an increase in adjusted margins due to improvements generated in its supply chain, product mix, and shedding lower margin SKUs. Overall, Utz posted gains in adjusted EBITDA, although its bottom-line income is held back by ongoing, largely accounting items resulting from its network transformation initiatives.

The results from the early part of 2025 continued the momentum. Organic growth came from the branded segment, which continued to lead market growth with its margin performance. Adjusted gross margin improved further, with ongoing productivity gains, whereas adjusted EBITDA outpaced growth in revenue, indicating better operating leverage. Although there is some noise in these figures due to transition costs, there is no doubt about progress on an underlying runway.

Capex remains high during the ongoing completion and optimization of major automation projects. Management front-loaded these expenditures, with expectations to slow down after 2025, which in turn should provide enough support for growth in their free cash flow. Leverage remains manageable, and management expects it to decrease as profitability improves and CapEx moderates. They have reaffirmed their expectations on growth in their organic revenue, adj. EBITDA, and double-digit growth in their adj. EPS.

Overall, the financial direction is now one of improving revenue quality, increasing profitability, and a business cycling out of its high investment stage into cleaner free cash flow.

Valuation: What Are You Paying for Quality?

Even with these gains in market share, Utz's market multiple reflects those usually found in maturing, no-growth packaged food companies. On a forward-looking multiple basis, its multiple based on normalized EPS is in low double-digit territory, with its EV/EBITDA multiple in forward years being at the low end of its nearest snack food competitors.

To provide context, companies such as those in packaged foods with more muted growth rates have traded on similar or higher multiples, while their larger snack companies have traded at premium multiples. By comparison, Utz is now in what could be described as unlocking new operating leverage, which is not yet factored into its valuation.

Using, what I believe to be, rather conservative assumptions: growth on the P&L to remain modest, EBITDA margins to improve incrementally, with CapEx increasing to maintenance levels, a ballpark figure for intrinsic value certainly is healthy upside from the current share price. Without having to assume full re-rating to sector multiples, there is, in my opinion, substantial discount to intrinsic value for such a stable demand environment, improving profitability, and dividend-paying company.

Company

Ticker

Forward P/E*

EV/EBITDA*

Comment

Utz Brands

UTZ

~ 12×-13× (normalised)

~ 10×-11×

Small-cap snack company; margin expansion underway

J&J Snack Foods

JJSF

~ 20-21×

—

Larger snack diversified company; higher multiple reflecting scale & broader business

TreeHouse Foods

THS

— (unprofitable)

~ 8×

Private-label focussed; lower growth and margin profile

Insiders and Sentiment

Insider activity is also an important indicator. Several senior officials purchased shares of the company at low points, showing confidence in the company’s direction. These were not token purchases, given that they took place while the market outlook was cautiously optimistic.

Institutional sentiment, on the other hand, remains tempered. Coverage is shallow for such a small food group company, and most generalist institutional funds have preferred staples with higher levels of liquidity. There is also a clear divergence between underlying institutional interest and the still-muted sentiment reflected in the share price.

Catalysts On the Horizon

Several things could drive changes in perception for investors. One would be EBITDA margin expansion. With operational improvements passing through, the market will have to think about the company’s core earnings opportunities. Another is geographic expansion. Success in California and other less penetrated markets will simply prove its nationalization plan is viable. Another factor would be normalized free cash flow, which reflects normalized capital expenditures. And, finally, there is industry consolidation – it could be an optional catalyst in several years, given its status as one of the few major independent salty snack food companies in the U.S. market.

Risks: Where Could This Break?

Competitive intensity remains the most pressing threat. Private labels and major brands exert pressure on pricing and out-of-stock situations in growth markets. Leverage remains a concern, though it is controlled, thereby curbing flexibility if growth momentum changes. The risk of execution also persists, given its ongoing transformation of its network over several years. Service levels for its retail partners could have an immediate impact in case there are operational issues. Though it has "Better for You Products," changes in consumer views on health could demand faster innovation.

Conclusion: Still Worth the Price?

It’s not difficult to see why Utz is transforming from a regional snack food company into a national platform with enhanced branding, optimized networks, and growing margins. Fundamentally, the market’s valuation remains stuck in the old model, rather than recognizing improvements occurring in the underlying business. Overall, with good execution on its plan, I believe there’s attractive risk-reward in its shares, given its underlying improvements and its depressed valuation, prompting me to rate Utz BUY.
